statement: Þórdís Kolbrún Reykfjörð Gylfadóttir, leader of Viðreisn (Reform Party), serves as utanríkisráðherra (Foreign Minister) in the coalition government formed in late 2024. She has been Viðreisn's leader since 2022 and was first elected to Althingi in 2016. As Foreign Minister, she has been the primary government spokesperson on the EU referendum process, including submitting the parliamentary resolution (þingsályktunartillaga) for the 29 August 2026 referendum. Her dual role as party leader of Iceland's most pro-EU party and as Foreign Minister responsible for EU relations has drawn criticism from opposition parties, who argue that the referendum process is being driven by partisan interests rather than national interest. Viðreisn has been the strongest parliamentary advocate for EU membership since the party's founding in 2016.
source_name: Althingi — Member directory; Government of Iceland cabinet listing
caveats: The combination of party leadership and the relevant ministerial portfolio is common in Icelandic coalition politics — it is standard practice for senior coalition party leaders to hold key ministerial positions aligned with their party's policy priorities.

related_entries.statement: Viðreisn (Reform Party) is led by Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir, who serves as utanríkisráðherra (Foreign Minister) in the current government. She is an MP for Suðvesturkjördæmi. Viðreisn was founded in 2014 (originally as a political movement, became a party in 2016) with a pro-EU platform as a core policy. The party holds 11 Althingi seats and consistently advocates for completing EU accession negotiations. Other prominent Viðreisn MPs include Pawel Bartoszek (Reykjavíkurkjördæmi norður), Jón Gnarr (Reykjavíkurkjördæmi suður), and Hanna Katrín Friðriksson (Reykjavíkurkjördæmi norður, also Minister of Industries).

The current Icelandic government is a coalition of Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, 13 seats), Viðreisn (Reform Party, 11 seats), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party, 8 seats), holding 32 of 63 Althingi seats. The Prime Minister is Kristrún Frostadóttir (Samfylkingin). This is the first government coalition where both explicitly pro-EU parties (Samfylkingin and Viðreisn) serve together, which has been cited as a factor in advancing the EU referendum.

The Icelandic government announced in early 2026 that a national referendum on resuming EU accession negotiations would be held on 29 August 2026. The referendum is based on a parliamentary resolution (þingsályktunartillaga) submitted by the Foreign Minister. The three coalition parties — Samfylkingin (Social Democrats), Viðreisn (Reform Party), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party) — support holding the referendum. The referendum question concerns whether Iceland should resume the accession negotiations that were suspended in 2013, not whether Iceland should join the EU outright. This is an advisory referendum (ráðgefandi þjóðaratkvæðagreiðsla), as Icelandic constitutional law does not provide for binding referenda on policy questions (the only binding referendum mechanism is under Article 26 for presidential vetoes of legislation).

citations.report_title: Ekki víst hvernig Evrópusambandið bregst við

claims.claim_text: Hugtakið upplýsingaóreiða hefur verið notað um umræðuna og sjónir beinast í auknum mæli að stjórnvöldum í þeim efnum.

citations.report_title: Iðnaðurinn forherðist í andstöðu gegn ESB

claims.claim_text: Ríkisstjórnin hefur lagt fram þingályktunartillögu um þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu um framhald aðildarviðræðna við ESB.
Þjóðaratkvæðagreiðsla um ESB-aðild hefur verið eitt helsta baráttumál Viðreisnar.
